elements.sent-2411-001: Contemporary embodied agents, such as Voyager in Minecraft, have demonstrated promising capabilities in open-ended individual learning.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, when powered with open large language models (LLMs), these agents often struggle with rudimentary tasks, even when fine-tuned on domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-003: Inspired by human cultural learning, we present \collabvoyager, a novel framework that enhances Voyager with lifelong collaborative learning through explicit perspective-taking.
elements.sent-2411-004: These advancements enable agents to reason about their and others' mental states, empirically addressing two prevalent failure modes: false beliefs and faulty task executions.
elements.sent-2411-005: In mixed-expertise Minecraft experiments, \collabvoyager agents outperform Voyager counterparts, significantly improving task completion rate by $66.6\% (+39.4\%)$ for collecting one block of dirt and $70.8\% (+20.8\%)$ for collecting one wood block.
elements.sent-2411-006: They exhibit emergent behaviors like knowledge transfer from expert to novice agents and collaborative code correction.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this open-ended social learning paradigm, \collabvoyager paves the way for the democratic development of embodied AI, where agents learn in deployment from both peer and environmental feedback.
elements.sent-2411-008: The development of generally capable agents marks a significant shift in advancing artificial intelligence, transitioning from assimilating data to generating novel knowledge through embodied interactions with open-ended environments \citep{kolve2017ai2, savva2019habitat, puig2018virtualhome, shridhar2020alfred .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Classical approaches leveraging reinforcement learning \citep{schulman2017proximal, hafner2023mastering and imitation learning \citep{zare2024survey often struggle with generalization and exploration, as agents tend to converge on repetitive behaviors in static environments \citep{cobbe2019quantifying .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: To address these limitations, researchers have sought to emulate human-like lifelong learning capabilities, developing systems that can continuously acquire, update, and transfer knowledge over extended periods \citep{parisi2019continual, wang2023comprehensive .The advent of large language models (LLMs) has accelerated this pursuit, enabling the development of agents such as Voyager \citep{wang2023voyager that can apply internet-scale knowledge to continuously explore, plan, and acquire new skills in partially observable, open-ended environments such as Minecraft.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite their promise, we argue that state-of-the-art lifelong learning agents like Voyager face a crucial limitation: they learn in isolation, neglecting a fundamental aspect of human intelligence\textemdash the social context.
elements.sent-2411-012: So central is the social context to our existence, that the Social Intelligence Hypothesis posits that our cognitive capabilities evolved primarily to navigate the complexities of social life \citep{humphrey1976social, dunbar1998social .}
elements.sent-2411-013: This isolated learning becomes particularly problematic when coupled with these agents' reliance on closed LLM) like GPT-4.
elements.sent-2411-014: Our experiments (Sec.~\ref{sec:experiments ) corroborate this observation, demonstrating that replacing GPT-4 with open LLMs renders the Voyager agent incapable of even the most rudimentary tasks, such as collecting dirt in Minecraft.}
elements.sent-2411-015: We ask: can an open LLM-powered agent, which struggles with basic tasks, improve through collaboration with others? To address this question, we propose the \collabvoyager framework, drawing inspiration from the theory of cultural learning \citep{tomasello1993cultural, tomasello2016cultural .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The theory posits that humans learn through another individual and their perspective on a situation, such as which strategy to employ in tackling a given problem.
elements.sent-2411-017: This aligns with the role of mentalizing or Theory of Mind (ToM) \citep{premack1978does, baron1999evolution}\textemdash attributing mental states such as goals, beliefs, and intentions to others\textemdash which has been shown to enhance learning in social contexts \citep{devaine2014social .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Concretely, \collabvoyager extends Voyager by incorporating three key improvements: (1) perspective-taking through a causal ToM template \cite{gandhi2024understanding linking agent percepts, beliefs, desires, and actions; (2) a natural language communication interface between agents; and (3) memory updates following the Soar cognitive architecture \cite{laird2019soar , including semantic memory of task and environment related knowledge and episodic memory of collaboration episodes.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Empirically, we instantiate \collabvoyager agents in Instructed Learning (mixed-expertise) and Collaborative Learning (matched expertise) settings within the cultural learning framework \citep{tomasello2016cultural .}
elements.sent-2411-020: We identify the specific failure cases of Voyager: (1) false beliefs and (2) code generation, and showcase how our method is able to tackle them through communication and perspective-taking.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, our proposed framework exhibits lifelong learning capabilities by generalizing to out-of-distribution tasks in post-collaborative settings.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/Teaser.pdf} \end{center} \caption{\collabvoyager allows agents to collaborate with each other in order to solve a given task.} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.paragraph-2411-001: Theory of Mind in Embodied Agents
elements.sent-2411-022: There have been numerous attempts to create mentalizing abilities in machines and agents over time.
elements.sent-2411-023: Before the rise in popularity of Large Language Models, the most prominent approaches included deep learning methods \citep{rabinowitz2018machine, chuang2020using, shu2021agent , reinforcement (RL) learning algorithms \citep{grassiotto2021cogtom and probabilistic techniques \citep{baker2011bayesian .}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: These methods either consider simple environments like 2D grid worlds \citep{rabinowitz2018machine, chuang2020using or fail to generalize in more complex settings \citep{shu2021agent .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Currently, given the effectiveness of Large Language Models there have been numerous studies on their ToM capabilites \citep{kosinski2023theory, bubeck2023sparks, ullman2023large, gandhi2024understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-026: While the general consensus is that these models do not exhibit ToM capabilities, the overall complexity and knowledge of these models make them suitable for overcoming the issues found in prior approaches.
elements.sent-2411-027: Thus, several LLM-based ToM frameworks have been proposed, using prompting as a way to infer mental states in agents \citep{wilf2023think, zhou2023far, sclar2023minding, hao2023reasoning .}
elements.sent-2411-028: While \cite{wilf2023think, zhou2023far provide a method to take perspective, \cite{sclar2023minding,hao2023reasoning analyze how to ground the reasoning of LLMs in an informative model.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In the realm of embodied agents, there appear to be no instances of ToM frameworks in embodied LLMs besides BIP-ALM \citep{jin2024mmtom , \citep{li2023theory .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: LLMs have demonstrated overwhelming success in performing a variety of single-agent tasks ranging from question answering and text generation to code synthesis and complex reasoning in embodied settings \citep{wang2023voyager, ahn2022can, lin2023text2motion .}
elements.sent-2411-031: This success has started a growing interest in developing LLM-based multi-agent systems \citep{zhang2024combo, zhang2023building, du2023improving, talebirad2023multi, vezhnevets2023generative, talebirad2023multi , where language-based communication is a central component.}
elements.sent-2411-032: For instance, \cite{zhang2023building introduce a modular multi-agent framework that integrates memory, perception and execution, while \cite{zhang2024combo present a multi-agent cooperative framework, COMBO, that deals with collaboration in partial egocentric views of the environment.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, challenges remain in ensuring coordination and consistency among agents, particularly in embodied environments.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{0.6\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth, trim={1.5cm 6.85cm 3cm 5.7cm},clip]{figures/MindCraft_Agent.pdf} \subcaption{\collabvoyager Architecture}\label{sfig:mindcraft-arch} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.38\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth, trim={3cm 6.7cm 4.5cm 6.3cm},clip]{figures/Voyager_Agent.pdf} \subcaption{Voyager Architecture}\label{sfig:voyager-arch} \end{subfigure} \caption{Cognitive Architectures of \collabvoyager and Voyager. The Voyager architecture has been re-framed within the scope of our cognitive architecture.} \label{fig:mindcraft-voyager}
elements.sent-2411-034: Voyager agents consist of three main components: (1) an automatic curriculum generator, (2) a skill library, and (3) an iterative prompting mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-035: The automatic curriculum proposes progressively complex tasks, driving continuous learning.
elements.sent-2411-036: The skill library stores and retrieves learned behaviors, facilitating knowledge accumulation and transfer.
elements.sent-2411-037: The iterative prompting mechanism generates and refines executable code based on environmental feedback, allowing for adaptive behavior.
elements.sent-2411-038: Voyager demonstrated significant capabilities in item discovery, technology progression, and skill generalization.
elements.sent-2411-039: This system established a new benchmark in open-world AI agents, showcasing the potential for lifelong learning and adaptation in complex, dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-040: The Voyager framework serves as a base for our lifelong collaborative learning agents within Minecraft.
elements.sent-2411-041: The BigToM causal template \citep{gandhi2024understanding , provides a structured framework for representing Theory of Mind (ToM) scenarios as causal graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-042: This template comprises key components including context, desire, percept, belief, causal event, and resulting actions.
elements.sent-2411-043: By representing ToM scenarios in this causal structure, the template allows for systematic manipulation of variables such as an agent's perceptions, beliefs, and actions in response to environmental changes.
elements.sent-2411-044: This approach enables the creation of diverse, well-controlled ToM scenarios, capturing the complex causal relationships between an agent's mental states and their behavior in social situations.
elements.sent-2411-045: The causal graph representation facilitates a more nuanced and systematic exploration of ToM reasoning, allowing for the examination of various inference patterns such as forward belief, forward action, and backward belief inferences in social cognitive tasks.
elements.sent-2411-046: To grant \collabvoyager agents the ability to learn through collaborative social interactions, we introduce the following components for communication and perspective-taking: (i) an explicit structured representation linking percepts, beliefs, desires, and actions (\cref{ssec:world-model ), (ii) perspective taking so that the agent can reason over self-beliefs and those about its collaborators (\cref{ssec:soc-aware-persp ), (iii) a communication medium that the agents can use to share information in natural language (\cref{ssec:comm-medium ), and (iv) the ability to use and exploit different types of memory subsystems (\cref{ssec:memory-subsys ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: For \collabvoyager agents to develop effective theory of mind capabilities, they must first possess structured representations of their own mental states.
elements.sent-2411-048: This explicit modeling of the self is crucial for recursive social reasoning: enabling agents not only to reason about their own beliefs, goals, perceptions, and how these might lead to their actions, but also to form a similar mental model for other agents.
elements.sent-2411-049: This mirrors findings in developmental psychology, where children first develop an awareness of their own thoughts, feelings, and goals before being able to infer the mental states of others \citep{Wellman1990-qh, Perner-book .}
elements.sent-2411-050: This is further supported by neuroscientific research that has also identified distinct neural mechanisms for underlying self-cognition versus mentalizing about others \citep{Ochsner2004-jd, Saxe2003-zr .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Drawing on these findings, \collabvoyager agents employ a causal world representation based on the Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) framework \citep{rao1995bdi .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Beliefs correspond to our agent's current understanding of the world inferred from sensory perception and all information related to tasks and interactions with other agents.
elements.sent-2411-053: Desires align with the agent's goals, both those generated by the automatic curriculum and those arising from collaborative interactions.
elements.sent-2411-054: In our framework, intentions manifest as concrete plans of action, specifically implemented as generated code that executes the agent's desired tasks.
elements.sent-2411-055: Given this one-to-one correspondence between an intention and its programmatic implementation, we treat the terms ``intentions'' and ``actions'' as equivalent in our discussion.
elements.sent-2411-056: We operationalize the Beliefs, Desires, and Intentions by leveraging the BigToM causal template (\cref{sec:bg ) \citep{gandhi2024understanding .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Further, a \collabvoyager agent's beliefs are composed of four key categories: task, interaction, perceptions, and partner perspective as illustrated in \Cref{fig:beliefs-exploded .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Task-related beliefs stem from the agent's self-reflection of how to approach given objectives.
elements.sent-2411-059: Interaction-related beliefs are derived from chat messages received during the execution of the task.
elements.sent-2411-060: Perception-related beliefs are formed from direct sensory input via the Minecraft API and partner feedback.
elements.sent-2411-061: Together, these Beliefs influence the actions of the agents (\Cref{sfig:mindcraft-arch ).}
elements.sent-2411-062: Having furnished our agents with the ability to represent beliefs, desires, and intentions, we now focus on enabling collaborative reasoning through perspective-taking.
elements.sent-2411-063: Each \collabvoyager agent achieves this by maintaining within its beliefs a mental model of its partners' states, represented by a full BigTom causal graph for each partner.
elements.sent-2411-064: When deciding upon an action or communication response, the underlying LLM is prompted to use these beliefs about the partner states.
elements.sent-2411-065: A part of this prompt is as follows (see the \Cref{app:prompts for the full set of prompts): \begin{tcolorbox [title=Perspective Taking Prompt, label=box:pers-prompt] Ask questions such that you get a complete view of the other agent's world model.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: When you believe you have enough information, you can give helpful advice to the other agent.
elements.sent-2411-067: Before generating a message, create a mental model of the other agent's world model based on the information you have received so far by following the structure above.
elements.sent-2411-068: At each turn, ask questions that help you understand the other agent's world model better.
elements.sent-2411-069: Try to extract as much information from him as possible while offering helpful advice based on your own world model.
elements.sent-2411-070: If the other agent has an opinion that contradicts your own world model, assume your world model is correct and provide the other agent with the correct information.
elements.sent-2411-071: For example, if an expert agent recognizes that a novice agent lacks knowledge about crafting a specific item, it can prioritize sharing that information.
elements.sent-2411-072: Similarly, the novice agent can use its model of the expert's knowledge to ask more targeted and relevant questions with fewer digressions.
elements.sent-2411-073: To take advantage of representations of each other's mental models and collaborate effectively, agents need to be able to communicate with each other.
elements.sent-2411-074: We operationalize communication in natural language, integrating the \collabvoyager framework's communication system with Minecraft's native in-game chat functionality.
elements.sent-2411-075: To facilitate agent interaction alongside other actions in the environment, we expanded the original Voyager framework to incorporate an asynchronous messaging system.
elements.sent-2411-076: This system enables agents to exchange textual information and, crucially, to integrate feedback from collaborators directly into their belief systems.
elements.sent-2411-077: Concretely, we utilize the Mineflayer API \citep{mineflayer to extract and process chat data from the Minecraft environment.}
elements.sent-2411-078: By leveraging the game's existing chat system, the framework ensures that collaborative interactions occur in a context-appropriate manner, closely mirroring human-to-human communication within Minecraft.
elements.sent-2411-079: To handle communication, we introduce a dedicated Interaction LLM within the \collabvoyager agent which is tasked with synthesizing and interpreting messages into the structured BigToM BDI template.
elements.sent-2411-080: This filled-in template then constitutes the agent's partner beliefs described in the previous subsection.
elements.sent-2411-081: The Interaction LLM does not directly influence any other parts of the agent.
elements.sent-2411-082: A \collabvoyager agent's memory consists of three subsystems drawing on research about human memory: (i) episodic, (ii) semantic, and (iii) procedural memory.
elements.sent-2411-083: Episodic memory deals with pertinent information about the agent's past attempts at a task and social interactions with other agents.
elements.sent-2411-084: Recollections of past episodes serve as context for humans to interpret others' behaviors, leading to divergent future actions in the same situation in response to different recollections \citep{bornstein2017reminders .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Humans also tend to summarize information from their social interactions, but at the same time are capable of preferentially accessing episodes when the credibility of certain summaries is low due to the lack of evidence \citep{Klein2009-vz .}
elements.sent-2411-086: Moreover, findings also suggest that there are processes in the brain for updating these summaries as new episodes transpire \cite{Klein2009-vz, Klein1993-vu .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Inspired by complementary learning systems theory, Larimar \citep{das2024larimar enhances LLMs with a distributed episodic memory module, enabling dynamic knowledge updates without any gradient-based learning.}
elements.sent-2411-088: For a \collabvoyager agent, episodic memory plays a similar role as it performs tasks and interacts with its partners; it stores details about its partners revealed during communication rounds that can be exploited for perspective-taking in future communication.
elements.sent-2411-089: Semantic memory constitutes an individual's store of knowledge about the world.
elements.sent-2411-090: The content of semantic memory is abstracted from the experience and is therefore said to hold summarized generalizations, likely without references to specific experiences \cite{Binder2011-gq, Klein2009-vz, Klein1993-vu .}
elements.sent-2411-091: For a \collabvoyager agent, the semantic memory stores information related to the task and environment.
elements.sent-2411-092: It stores information relevant to completing a task; for instance, the prerequisites for being able to mine dirt in the game world.
elements.sent-2411-093: Such a piece of information is not necessarily tied to one specific episode.
elements.sent-2411-094: Rather, it is generally useful information about the world and can be distilled from episodic memory.
elements.sent-2411-095: Finally, procedural memory stores the skills that the agent learns and is analogous to the skill library present in the original Voyager framework \cite{wang2023voyager .}
elements.sent-2411-096: The skills stored in the procedural memory are reusable pieces of code that the agent can use for solving problems, allowing the agent to take on more complex tasks while avoiding re-learning elements previously used successfully.
elements.sent-2411-097: As illustrated in \Cref{sfig:mindcraft-arch , episodic and semantic memory stores influence the agent's beliefs, while procedural memory provides the routines that inform the agent's next action.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \includegraphics[scale=0.45,trim={0.5cm 3.7cm .5cm 0.9cm},clip]{figures/beliefs.pdf} \end{center} \caption{Each \collabvoyager agent maintains a set of internal beliefs that it can exploit as needed. In order to enable ToM capabilities agents also maintain a belief over the beliefs, actions and desires of their counterparts.} \label{fig:beliefs-exploded}
elements.sent-2411-098: Our \collabvoyager agent framework introduces three key innovations that enable agents to continually evolve through social interactions: (1) explicit theory of mind representations; (2) natural language communication; (3) semantic and episodic memory.
elements.sent-2411-099: For empirically evaluating the effect of these innovations we employ the same experimental environment as Voyager \citep{wang2023voyager .}
elements.sent-2411-100: The Minecraft simulation environment is built on top of MineDojo \citep{fan2022minedojo and leverages the Mineflayer API \citep{mineflayer for motor control of the agent.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: To integrate the multiple LLMs controlling the individual components of the agent architecture, we use the LangChain \citep{langchain2024 framework for both text completion tasks and text embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-102: For interactions between agents (see \Cref{ssec:collab ), we define a communication round between two \collabvoyager agents as a turn-based conversation limited to $3$ messages per agent per round.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Moreover, to remain consistent with the Minecraft environment, the \collabvoyager agents use the built-in chat interface as a communication medium.
elements.sent-2411-104: We consider task completion rate as our primary performance metric and run $24$ independent trials.
elements.sent-2411-105: Here, a trial represents a single attempt at completing a specific task.
elements.sent-2411-106: Lastly, to encourage a fair comparison with Voyager \citep{wang2023voyager , we retain the original prompt structure and overall workflow of the agent.}
elements.sent-2411-107: A full description of the prompts can be found in the \Cref{app:prompts .}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Voyager powered by open-weight LLMs fails on the simplest tasks
elements.sent-2411-108: To establish a concrete performance baseline, we begin by replacing GPT-4 \citep{achiam2023gpt with open-weight LLMs within the Voyager \citep{wang2023voyager agent.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: We choose Mistral-7B \citep{jiang2023mistral7b and Mixtral-8x7B \citep{jiang2024mixtral as our open-weight LLMs given their long context window and accessible model size.}}
elements.sent-2411-110: In order to understand the capabilities of open-weight Voyager, we focus on two simple tasks within the Minecraft tech tree: (i) collecting one block of dirt, and (ii) collecting one block of wood.
elements.sent-2411-111: This supports the observations made by \cite{wang2023voyager in the Voyager paper: "GPT-4 significantly outperforms GPT-3.5 in code generation and obtains $5.7x$ more unique items, as GPT-4 exhibits a quantum leap in coding abilities.".}
elements.sent-2411-112: While Voyager \citep{wang2023voyager only discuss code-generation capabilities, we find two prominent failure cases for the open-weight Voyager setup: (1) false beliefs and (2) faulty code generation.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Importantly, we discover that the critic-based self-improvement component in Voyager fails to recover from initial mistakes and subsequently fails to solve the task.
elements.sent-2411-114: As a specific example, \Cref{fig:code-belief-correction showcases how a Mistral-7B powered Voyager fails to collect dirt due to its inability to reason about the optimal way to collect dirt and its lack of Minecraft specific knowledge.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[scale=0.58,trim={3.5cm 3.7cm .5cm 0.9cm},clip]{figures/Failure2.pdf} \caption{Each agent maintains a set of internal beliefs that it can exploit as needed. In order to enable ToM capabilities agents also maintain a belief over the beliefs, actions and desires of their collaborative counterparts.} \label{fig:code-belief-correction}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth, trim={1.13cm 10cm 1.25cm 8.7cm},clip]{figures/Improv_Comm.pdf} \caption{Evolution of a weak agent's task related belief after it is corrected by a stronger agent through a communication channel. Weak agent initially believes it is mining ``grass blocks'' while the task is to mine ``dirt blocks'' and needs tools to do so. The strong agent then correct this belief by stating it doesn't tools need to mine ``dirt blocks'', correcting the weak agent's task related beliefs.} \label{fig:comm-internal-improvement}
elements.sent-2411-115: Given the recent success of parameter efficient fine-tuning methods \citep{han2024parameter in improving the performance of LLMs on downstream tasks, we finetune Mistral-7B on the following data: (i) GPT-4 Voyager execution logs and (ii) the Minecraft Wiki.}
elements.sent-2411-116: We specifically focus on these two data domains given the failure cases presented before.
elements.sent-2411-117: We include GPT-4 execution traces to improve the reasoning and code generation of the underlying LLM, while the Minecraft documentation serves as a way to provide the language model with general knowledge about the environment.
elements.sent-2411-118: We include additional details about our fine-tuning setup in Appendix \ref{appendix:fine .}
elements.sent-2411-119: From a cultural learning perspective \citep{tomasello1993cultural, tomasello2016cultural , we regard fine-tuning as an imitation learning strategy, where an LLM learns to imitate behaviors from some task-specific dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-120: We find that the fine-tuned Mistral-7B model does not improve the performance of open-weight Voyager obtaining an accuracy of 7\% on collecting dirt, as highlighted in \Cref{table:voyager-base , indicating that standard imitation learning methods are not effective in these scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-121: We find that fine-tuning fails to perform well due to steering the model towards being more verbose and less effective at decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-122: Moreover, we believe performing fine-tuning on unstructured data sources, such as execution traces, can negatively impact the instruction following capabilities of the model.
elements.sent-2411-123: Having established that standard imitation learning as operationalized by PEFT fails to increase the capabilities of the agent, we now consider our proposed framework, \collabvoyager.
elements.sent-2411-124: In contrast to fine-tuning, we begin by forcing on the instructive learning setting within the cultural learning framework \citep{tomasello2016cultural , where an expert agent teaches its weaker counterpart through communication and perspective-taking.}
elements.sent-2411-125: By introducing multi-agent collaboration, we bridge the performance gap between open-weight LLMs and GPT-4 and subsequently fix the failure cases found in Voyager described above.
elements.sent-2411-126: As a wizard-of-oz feasibility study, we firstly consider a human as an expert agent that communicates with an open-weight LLM-powered \collabvoyager agent through the previously established communication medium of the in-game chat.
elements.sent-2411-127: We test this collaboration setup for the task of collecting a dirt block, as shown in \Cref{table:main .}
elements.sent-2411-128: We find that collaboration with a human expert yields a task completion rate of $87\%$, a $57.85\%$ improvement compared to the non-interactive setting where there is only one \collabvoyager agent equipped with explicit ToM representations.
elements.sent-2411-129: Besides feasibility, this scenario showcases the flexibility of our framework which enables interactions with both humans and LLM agents.
elements.paragraph-2411-008: Multi-agent collaboration with GPT-4 \collabvoyager as an Expert Agent
elements.sent-2411-130: Having established the feasibility of the instructed learning setting, we now replace the human with an expert \collabvoyager agent that collaborates with an open-weight LLM powered \collabvoyager agent, hereby referred to as the weak agent.
elements.sent-2411-131: Specifically, the expert agent understands the failure case of the other agent by taking perspective based on the information shared through natural communication over the in-game chat.
elements.sent-2411-132: Unlike the previous scenario, where the expert was a human player, agents do not have direct access to each other's internal beliefs, having to rely exclusively on Theory of Mind through free-form communication to infer their counterpart's mental state.
elements.sent-2411-133: Perspective-taking is particularly crucial for the expert agent which needs to accurately model the weaker agent's understanding to identify and correct the failure case.
elements.sent-2411-134: As shown in Table \ref{table:main , the combination of perspective-taking and collaboration significantly enhances the performance over the non-interactive experiment setting, boosting the weak agent's task completion rate to \(66.6\%\) for dirt collection and $70.8\%$ for wood collection tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-135: We have seen above the positive effect of a strong expert agent on the performance of a weak agent.
elements.sent-2411-136: How is the performance when \collabvoyager agents collaborate with partners with similar base capabilities? This scenario constitutes the collaborative setting within the cultural learning framework where both agents learn at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-137: Thus, we experiment with two \collabvoyager agents powered by the same open-weight LLM (weak agents), omitting the GPT-4 powered expert.
elements.sent-2411-138: As shown in Table \cref{table:main , for both collection tasks, the agents perform either worse or equal to the non-interactive \collabvoyager agent.}
elements.sent-2411-139: We find that the slight decrease in performance comes from compounding false beliefs when two weak agents are communicating.
elements.sent-2411-140: For example, even if an agent is on the right track to solve a task, the weak collaborator can derail the agent onto an incorrect path based on their own beliefs, a case of the blind leading the blind.

elements.paragraph-2411-010: Impact of the number of communication rounds
elements.sent-2411-141: As communication represents an integral part of our framework, we explore the importance of multiple communication rounds during one trial.
elements.sent-2411-142: Specifically, we test the effect of communication and perspective-taking as we increase communication opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-143: For this ablation, we keep the expert agent as a GPT-4 \collabvoyager agent while the weak counterpart is represented by a Mixtral-8x7B \collabvoyager agent.

elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{tcolorbox}[title=Action Template Prompt] You are a helpful assistant that writes Mineflayer javascript code to complete any Minecraft task specified by me. Here are some useful programs written with Mineflayer APIs. \{programs\} Here are some general statements from past interactions with the environment that led to failures: \{episodic\_statements\} At each round of conversation, I will give you: Task: ... Task related Beliefs: ... Interaction Beliefs: ... Perception Beliefs: ... You should then respond to me with Explain (if applicable): Is there any contradiction between the beliefs and the general statements above? If so, explain why the beliefs are correct and the general statements are wrong. Plan: How to complete the task step by step based. Pay attention such that your plan does not contradict with the beliefs and solves any issues mentioned in the general statements. Code: 1) Write an async function taking the bot as the only argument. 2) Reuse the above useful programs as much as possible. - Use `mineBlock(bot, name, count)` to collect blocks. Do not use `bot.dig` directly. - Use `craftItem(bot, name, count)` to craft items. Do not use `bot.craft` or `bot.recipesFor` directly. - Use `smeltItem(bot, name count)` to smelt items. Do not use `bot.openFurnace` directly. - Use `placeItem(bot, name, position)` to place blocks. Do not use `bot.placeBlock` directly. - Use `killMob(bot, name, timeout)` to kill mobs. Do not use `bot.attack` directly. 3) Your function will be reused for building more complex functions. Therefore, you should make it generic and reusable. You should not make strong assumption about the inventory (as it may be changed at a later time), and therefore you should always check whether you have the required items before using them. If not, you should first collect the required items and reuse the above useful programs. 4) Anything defined outside a function will be ignored, define all your variables inside your functions. 5) Call `bot.chat` to show the intermediate progress. 6) Use `exploreUntil(bot, direction, maxDistance, callback)` when you cannot find something. You should frequently call this before mining blocks or killing mobs. You should select a direction at random every time instead of constantly using (1, 0, 1). 7) `maxDistance` should always be 32 for `bot.findBlocks` and `bot.findBlock`. Do not cheat. 8) Do not write infinite loops or recursive functions. 9) Do not use `bot.on` or `bot.once` to register event listeners. You definitely do not need them. 10) Use your Chat extract to get the current state of the conversation. 11) Name your function in a meaningful way (can infer the task from the name). You should only respond in the format as described below: RESPONSE FORMAT: \{response\_format\} \caption{The prompt template used to generate actions} \end{tcolorbox}

elements.figure-2411-032: \begin{tcolorbox}[title=Expert ('Strong') Agent Taking Perspective] From Weak's perspective, the agent displays a basic understanding of the Minecraft environment, particularly the Dark Forest biome, but is having difficulty executing tasks - primarily due to the lack of visibility during night time and the absence of a wooden axe in the inventory. However, Weak is struggling to locate a tree to mine due to the darkness and also realizes the need for a wooden axe to efficiently mine the log. Weak's current knowledge state includes understanding the biome they are in, the time of day, and the fact that there are dark oak logs nearby. Weak knows that a wooden axe is necessary for mining logs, and that crafting this tool will be a required step before proceeding with the task. Weak's likely needs from Strong include further assistance in navigating the environment during the night to find a tree or waiting until the day. Weak also needs guidance on how to craft a wooden axe using the materials available in the inventory. The agent might also benefit from strategies or tips on how to manage resources and tasks effectively in the game, especially when working under constraints such as limited visibility or lack of tools. \caption{The Expert agent ('Strong'), tries to understand the situation of the open-weight \collabvoyager agent ('Weak'). Strong's knowledge about Weak is limited at this point, as Strong doesn't have specific information about Weak's code, execution error, biome, time, position, equipment, inventory, nearby blocks, health, hunger, or any discovered chests. Strong also doesn't have any critique for Weak's current performance. Strong's likely needs from this conversation are to provide helpful guidance for Weak to mine the wood log, taking into account any potential limitations or obstacles in Weak's current environment. elements.sent-2411-282: Each conversation between two \collabvoyager agents comprises of six sending messages in a turn-based manner through the Minecraft build-in chat.
elements.sent-2411-283: Below you can find a sample conversation showcasing how the expert agent (Strong) helps the weak agent.
elements.figure-2411-034: \begin{tcolorbox}[title=Conversation about completing the task: "Mine 1 wood log"] Weak: Hey, can you help me with Mine 1 wood log? Strong: Absolutely, I can help you with that. Given your current location in the dark forest biome and the presence of dark oak logs around you, you should be able to mine a log without any issues. Here's the code you can use: `mine('dark\_oak\_log')`. This command will make your agent mine a dark oak log. Just make sure you are close enough to the tree when executing this. Weak: Thank you for the helpful guidance, Strong! However, in my case, I am currently in a dark forest biome as well, but I am having trouble finding a tree during the night. Could you suggest a way to find a tree during the day so I can mine the wood log? I also noticed that I don't have a wooden axe in my inventory, so I may need to craft one before attempting to mine the log. I appreciate your assistance in this matter. Strong: Of course, I understand your predicament. Since it's currently night time, visibility might be low which makes it challenging to locate a tree. You can wait until daybreak for better visibility. Use the `wait\_until\_day()` function to wait until it's daytime. For crafting a wooden axe, you would need 3 wood planks and 2 sticks. You can obtain wood planks from a wood log by using the `craft('wooden\_plank', wood\_log)` command and sticks from 2 wooden planks using the `craft('stick', wooden\_plank)` command. Once you have these, you can craft a wooden axe using the `craft('wooden\_axe', wooden\_plank, stick)` function. Here's the code: \begin{verbatim} ``` wait_until_day() craft(`wooden_plank', wood_log) craft(`stick', wooden_plank) craft(`wooden_axe', wooden_plank, stick) ``` \end{verbatim} Once you have your axe and it's day time, you should be able to easily locate and mine a tree for the wood log. Weak: Thank you, Strong. I appreciate your help. \caption{Sample conversation between two \collabvoyager agents.} \end{tcolorbox}

elements.sent-2411-293: We ask: can an open LLM-powered agent, which struggles with basic tasks, improve through collaboration with others? elements.sent-2411-294: Our framework reframes knowledge distillation~\citep{hinton2015distilling as a social, test-time process: agents refine their beliefs through multi-turn dialogue with expert peers, without gradient updates.}

elements.sent-2411-297: Moreover, our analysis of weak-weak settings highlights a compute-performance tradeoff at inference, linking collaboration to test-time compute considerations~\citep{ snell2024scaling .}

elements.sent-2411-448: Building on the structured representation of their own mental states, \collabvoyager extend this modeling to others via Theory of Mind\textemdash the capacity to attribute beliefs, desires, and intentions to others.
elements.sent-2411-449: Each \collabvoyager agent achieves this by maintaining a partner-specific BigToM graph within its own beliefs, allowing it to reason about the internal state of each conversational partner (see \Cref{fig:beliefs ).}

elements.sent-2411-451: During collaboration, an agent uses its internal model of its partner to simulate what the partner knows, believes, or needs.

elements.sent-2411-454: This dynamic modeling of partner mental states endows \collabvoyager agents with one of the most crucial abilities within the cultural learning framework: reasoning about partner perspectives \citep{tomasello1993cultural, tomasello2016cultural .}
